pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Reverses horizontal inputs and vertical outputs.
misconceptions.error_signature: Reports a plotted point with its coordinates in the opposite order.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Name the horizontal input first and the vertical function value second.

misconceptions.description: Uses the wrong zero-coordinate test for an intercept.
misconceptions.error_signature: Calls a point an x-intercept when its x-coordinate, rather than its height, is zero.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: For an x-intercept set the height to zero; for a y-intercept set the input to zero.

misconceptions.description: Reads vertical change without keeping a consistent left-to-right direction.
misconceptions.error_signature: Rise and run are subtracted in opposite directions, producing the wrong sign.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Choose left and right endpoints once, then subtract right minus left for both coordinates.

metaphors.description: A function graph is a terrain route: an input names a horizontal location, the height names the output, and slope records the climb per horizontal step.

error_patterns: graph_intercept_axis_swap: tests the wrong coordinate for zero
graph_slope_direction_mismatch: rise and run use opposite subtraction orders
sources: OpenStax Precalculus 2e, Section 1.1: Functions and Function Notation.
OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 1.1: Review of Functions.

lesson_narrative.text: Read a graph from left to right: x is the input, f(x) is the height, and each exact point connects those two values.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: Slope is right y minus left y divided by right x minus left x.

lesson_narrative.text: Intercepts are zero-coordinate points, while increasing and decreasing intervals describe how height changes as x moves right.

remediation.text: Label the horizontal and vertical axes, then copy the exact endpoint coordinates before doing any arithmetic.

remediation.spoken_text: A plotted point is written as input first, output second.

remediation.text: For slope, draw the horizontal run and vertical rise separately and subtract both in the same direction.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Adds only some of the nonoverlapping regions.
misconceptions.error_signature: The reported total equals a proper subset of the listed rectangle and triangle areas.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Mark every region after its area has been included exactly once.

misconceptions.description: Treats a triangle as a full rectangle.
misconceptions.error_signature: Uses base times height without the one-half factor.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Pair the triangle with its matching rectangle and take half of that rectangle area.

misconceptions.description: Uses a listed dimension as the area or as an unrelated endpoint value.
misconceptions.error_signature: The answer copies a width or height without applying the shape formula.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Name the shape first, then write its complete area formula before substituting dimensions.

metaphors.description: Accumulated area is a tiled floor: partition the region into nonoverlapping familiar tiles, find each tile's area, and count every tile once.

error_patterns: area_region_omitted: one listed nonoverlapping shape is absent from the sum
area_triangle_not_halved: base times height is used as the triangle area
area_dimension_copied: a width or height is reported without applying an area formula
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.1: Approximating Areas.
OpenStax Precalculus 2e, Section 1.6: Absolute Value Functions.

lesson_narrative.text: For a nonnegative piecewise-linear region, partition the space under the graph into rectangles and triangles.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: Rectangle area is width times height; triangle area is base times height divided by two.

lesson_narrative.text: Because the pieces do not overlap and stay above the axis, the total area is the ordinary sum of their areas.

remediation.text: Sketch a boundary around each rectangle or triangle and write one formula beside each piece.

remediation.expression: total area=sum of nonoverlapping piece areas
remediation.spoken_text: Total area is the sum of all nonoverlapping piece areas.

remediation.text: Check off each piece after adding it and verify that every triangle contribution includes one-half.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Chooses heights from the wrong side of each subinterval.
misconceptions.error_signature: A left sum uses right endpoints, or a right sum uses left endpoints.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Write each subinterval first and circle the endpoint named by the method.

misconceptions.description: Adds heights but does not turn them into rectangle areas.
misconceptions.error_signature: The reported value is the sum of heights without the common-width factor.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Write width times height for every rectangle before adding.

misconceptions.description: Treats a midpoint sample as an endpoint sample.
misconceptions.error_signature: The selected x-values sit at subinterval edges rather than their centers.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Mark both endpoints of each subinterval, then locate the point halfway between them.

metaphors.description: A Riemann sum is a row of equal-width fence panels: the method chooses each panel's height, and width times height gives its contribution.

error_patterns: riemann_wrong_endpoint: samples the opposite edge of each subinterval
riemann_width_omitted: adds heights without multiplying by common width
riemann_midpoint_shift: uses an edge in place of the subinterval center
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.1: Approximating Areas.
OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.2: The Definite Integral.

lesson_narrative.text: A Riemann sum partitions an interval into equal widths and assigns one sampled height to each rectangle.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: Each rectangle contribution is the common width times its selected height.

lesson_narrative.text: Left, right, and midpoint methods differ only in which sample supplies each height.

remediation.text: List the subintervals in order and write L, R, or M beside the selected point for each one.

remediation.spoken_text: The rectangle sum is width times the sum of all selected heights.

remediation.text: Count selected heights and subintervals; the counts must match before you multiply.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Treats reversed bounds as the same direction.
misconceptions.error_signature: The value keeps its sign when the starting and ending bounds are exchanged.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Read the lower written bound as the start and negate the value when direction reverses.

misconceptions.description: Counts every geometric region as positive.
misconceptions.error_signature: Areas below the horizontal axis are added by magnitude instead of contributing negatively.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Attach a positive sign above the axis and a negative sign below it before adding.

misconceptions.description: Combines adjacent intervals with subtraction or drops one piece.
misconceptions.error_signature: The whole-interval value is not the sum of the two consistently oriented pieces.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Write the two adjacent intervals end to end and add their signed values.

metaphors.description: A definite integral is a directed trip ledger: contributions in the chosen direction carry signs, adjacent trip segments add, and reversing the trip reverses the total.

error_patterns: definite_bounds_reversed_without_sign: direction changes but sign does not
definite_unsigned_area: negative contributions are replaced by magnitudes
definite_additivity_break: adjacent signed values are not added
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.2: The Definite Integral.
OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.3: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

lesson_narrative.text: A definite integral is signed accumulation from its starting bound to its ending bound.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The integral from b to a equals the negative of the integral from a to b.

lesson_narrative.text: Adjacent intervals add when their directions agree; contributions below the axis remain negative.

remediation.text: Write an arrow from the starting bound to the ending bound and keep that direction through every piece.

remediation.spoken_text: The whole signed accumulation equals the left piece plus the right piece.

remediation.text: Before adding, label each region or supplied value with its sign and verify that adjacent endpoints meet.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Changes a coefficient but leaves the power unchanged.
misconceptions.error_signature: Differentiating the proposed term produces a power one lower than the required term.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Raise the exponent by one before adjusting the coefficient.

misconceptions.description: Multiplies by the new exponent instead of dividing by it.
misconceptions.error_signature: The derivative of the proposed term has an extra factor of the new exponent.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: After raising the power, divide the old coefficient by that new power.

misconceptions.description: Antidifferentiates only part of a polynomial.
misconceptions.error_signature: Differentiating the proposed result fails to reproduce one given term.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Process every polynomial term separately, then combine the results.

metaphors.description: Differentiation is a machine with a reversible polynomial setting: an antiderivative runs each power-rule step backward, while an arbitrary constant records what differentiation cannot recover.

error_patterns: antiderivative_power_unchanged: output exponent does not increase
antiderivative_coefficient_multiplied: coefficient is multiplied by the new exponent
antiderivative_term_dropped: derivative of the response omits a source term
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.3: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

lesson_narrative.text: An antiderivative is checked by differentiation, so each polynomial term reverses one power-rule step.

lesson_narrative.expression: new power=old power+1; new coefficient=old coefficient/new power
lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The new power is the old power plus one, and the new coefficient is the old coefficient divided by the new power.

lesson_narrative.text: One arbitrary constant is included because every constant has derivative zero.

remediation.text: Make a two-column table with each derivative term on the left and its reversed power-rule term on the right.

remediation.expression: differentiate the candidate -> recover the given polynomial
remediation.spoken_text: Differentiate the candidate and confirm that it recovers the given polynomial.

remediation.text: If the check fails, compare exponents first, then coefficients, and finally confirm that no term was omitted.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Adds the two antiderivative endpoint values.
misconceptions.error_signature: The result uses ending value plus starting value instead of their directed difference.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Write ending value minus starting value before substituting either number.

misconceptions.description: Subtracts endpoint values in the opposite order.
misconceptions.error_signature: The response is the negative of the correctly oriented result.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Match the first evaluation to the upper bound and subtract the evaluation at the lower bound.

misconceptions.description: Evaluates the integrand at the endpoints instead of an antiderivative.
misconceptions.error_signature: The work substitutes bounds into f rather than into a function whose derivative is f.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Name and verify an antiderivative F before evaluating either endpoint.

metaphors.description: An antiderivative acts like an accumulation odometer: the change in its reading from the starting bound to the ending bound gives the accumulated amount between them.

error_patterns: ftc_endpoint_addition: endpoint antiderivative values are added
ftc_subtraction_reversed: lower value is reduced by upper value
ftc_integrand_substitution: bounds are substituted into the integrand
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 5.3: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

lesson_narrative.text: The Fundamental Theorem turns a definite integral into an endpoint change in any antiderivative of the integrand.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The integral from a to b of f of x equals F of b minus F of a.

lesson_narrative.text: The antiderivative supplies F, and the written bound order determines ending value minus starting value.

remediation.text: Separate the work into three lines: identify F, evaluate F at the ending bound, then subtract F at the starting bound.

remediation.expression: endpoint change=ending antiderivative value-starting antiderivative value
remediation.spoken_text: Endpoint change is ending antiderivative value minus starting antiderivative value.

remediation.text: Differentiate F once to verify the integrand before using either endpoint.
